title: The cell fluid model with Curie-Weiss interactions: special cases and analytical results
authors: O. A. Dobush, M. P. Kozlovskii, I. V. Pylyuk, R. V. Romanik, M. A. Shpot
abstract: Inspired by previous extensive numerical studies of a cell fluid model with Curie-Weiss interactions, we concentrate on some analytically tractable special cases in its description. The key ingredient of the model is a competition between global attraction and local repulsion interactions between particles with coupling constants $J_1$ and $J_2$, respectively. We provide analytical results in several limiting cases, including the ideal-gas limit $J_1=J_2=0$ and the strong-repulsion limit $J_2\gg J_1$. For $J_2\gg J_1$, a detailed analytical study is presented. We derive explicit expressions for the critical point parameters, the equation of state, and the binodal and spinodal curves in closed form. The equation of state is found to be in full agreement with that of the van der Waals lattice gas, and the order parameter satisfies the standard Curie-Weiss equation. In a neighborhood of the critical point, a Landau expansion is shown to have the same form and symmetry as that of the classical lattice gas within the mean-field approximation. Moreover, based on the explicit knowledge of a few leading terms in the asymptotic expansion of the deformed exponential function governing the physics of the cell model, we extend its validity range to include the marginal case of thermodynamic stability, $J_1=J_2$. In particular, this extension makes a consideration of the ideal-gas limit $J_1=J_2=0$ formally legitimate. For the generic marginal case $J_1=J_2\ne0$ systematically avoided in previous works, we present numerical data and phase diagrams that augment their findings for $J_2>J_1$.
summary: This paper provides the first explicit analytical solutions for the phase behavior of a cell fluid model with competing long-range attraction and short-range repulsion. It establishes that the model recovers van der Waals-like behavior in certain limits and proves the mathematical stability of the system even at the boundary of thermodynamic collapse.
main_problem: The paper seeks to provide explicit analytical results for the Cell Fluid Model (CFM) with Curie-Weiss interactions, specifically addressing the lack of analytical descriptions for phase behavior, critical parameters, and the convergence of the partition function at the boundary of thermodynamic stability.
main_result: The authors derive closed-form expressions for the equation of state, critical points, and phase curves (binodal/spinodal) in the strong-repulsion limit, and prove that the partition function remains convergent at the marginal stability boundary (J1 = J2).
method: The study employs a grand-canonical ensemble approach using Laplace's method, asymptotic expansions of deformed exponential functions, and Landau expansion near the critical point.
model_or_system: A Cell Fluid Model (CFM) where a macroscopic volume is divided into non-overlapping cubic cells. The Hamiltonian features a competition between global Curie-Weiss attraction (J1) and local, short-range, soft-core repulsion (J2) between particles in the same cell.
key_observables: Equation of state, critical point parameters (Tc, Pc, density), binodal and spinodal curves, order parameter, and pressure isotherms.
important_parameters: Coupling constants J1 (attraction) and J2 (repulsion); the ratio f = J2/J1; temperature T; and chemical potential mu.
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on the thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity) and utilizes the large-f (strong-repulsion) approximation for several analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Phase diagrams showing coexistence lines and spinodal curves (P-T and density-T planes), pressure isotherms illustrating van der Waals loops, and plots of the energy function E(y) demonstrating the transition from single to double maxima.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the model definition and stability conditions, moves to the analytical derivation of the strong-repulsion limit and critical parameters, extends the analysis to the marginal stability case (J1=J2) via asymptotic expansion, and concludes with the construction of the full thermodynamic equation of state.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2: The pP ˚, ¯nq phase diagram of the cell fluid model with equal attraction and repulsion parameters, J1 “ J2, showing pressure isotherms (solid lines) at ten values of the reduced temperature T listed in the legend. The first two coexistence lines (binodals) related to the three lowest-density phases are indicated by dotted curves. Their apex points correspond to the critical points labelled by k “ 1 and k “ 2 in Table 1. The horizontal segments of the isotherms in the subcritical region indicate discontinuous density jumps accompanying the first- order phase transitions.
